title: Gajar Ka Halwa

ingredients.title: Ingredients
items: 500 g Fresh red Delhi carrots (peeled & grated)
½ tsp Green cardamom pods (freshly ground)

instruction.title: Step 1: Prep
steps: Peel and grate carrots using a medium-fine grater (avoid the food processor’s coarse blade; finer grating means silkier halwa).
Warm the saffron in 20 ml milk for later infusion.

instruction.title: Step 2: Slow-Cook the Carrots
steps: In a thick-bottomed heavy pan (copper or heavy stainless preferred), add grated carrots and full-fat milk.
Bring to a gentle boil, stirring occasionally.
Lower heat and simmer uncovered for 45–60 minutes, stirring every 4–5 minutes, until the milk is reduced by 70% and carrots have softened deeply.

instruction.title: Step 3: Enrich & Reduce
steps: Add khoya, stir well to incorporate.
Continue cooking on low heat for another 20–25 minutes until the mixture is thick and glossy.

instruction.title: Step 4: Ghee & Aromatics
steps: In a separate pan, heat ghee until aromatic.
Add the carrot mixture into the ghee and sauté on medium-low for 12–15 minutes, allowing the carrots to absorb the ghee fully — this is key to a rich, nutty flavor.
Stir in heavy cream for luxury mouthfeel.

instruction.title: Step 5: Sweeten & Finish
steps: Add sugar, cardamom powder, and saffron-infused milk.
Sugar should be added towards the end so the carrots retain texture.
Stir continuously for 8–10 minutes until the halwa leaves the sides of the pan and takes on a glossy sheen.

instruction.title: Step 6: Garnish & Serve
steps: Fry almonds, pistachios, and raisins in 1 tsp ghee until lightly golden; fold into the halwa, reserving some for garnish.
Serve warm in small copper katoris or porcelain ramekins, topped with a few strands of saffron and edible silver leaf (varq) for restaurant presentation.

chefTips: Carrot Choice: Use Delhi red carrots in winter for natural sweetness and color; avoid woody cores.
Ghee Timing: Adding ghee late in the process prevents it from separating and keeps halwa moist, not greasy.
Khoya Quality: Use fresh, unsweetened khoya for depth — avoid milk powder substitutes in fine dining.
Resting Time: Let halwa rest for at least 30 minutes before serving; flavors mature beautifully.
Serving Suggestion: Pair with warm malai kulfi, or serve in tasting portions for dessert platters.
